title: East Wing

jokes.text: Did he dye his mustache? / Holy shit, he dyed his mustache.
jokes.setup: A briefing is beginning about the president's first state visit — ostensibly serious political business.
jokes.punchline: Selina and a staffer derail the briefing immediately to fixate on whether the press secretary dyed his mustache.
jokes.explanation: Sharp cold open that establishes the show's rhythm instantly — serious framing demolished by petty vanity observation. The double-take structure (question, then confirmation) is economical. Character-specific in that it shows how the room's attention is always on the wrong thing.

jokes.text: It's vanity. He's the TV face of the administration. But he's glued Cheetos to that face.
jokes.setup: Someone defends Mike's vanity as understandable given his TV role.
jokes.punchline: The mustache is so absurdly orange it looks like Cheetos glued on.
jokes.explanation: Extremely tight construction. 'He's the TV face / he's glued Cheetos to that face' is a near-perfect pivot — sets up a reasonable defense then obliterates it with a perfectly specific, visual insult. Economy is exceptional at 9 words of punchline for maximum imagery.

jokes.text: He looks like Yosemite Sam. Yeah, without the credibility.
jokes.setup: The Cheetos/mustache insults have been escalating.
jokes.punchline: Yosemite Sam is already a ridiculous comparison; the 'without the credibility' kicker implies Sam has MORE credibility than Mike.
jokes.explanation: The 'without the credibility' tag is the best line in the cold open. It's a double-joke: Yosemite Sam is funny on its own, but adding that Sam has MORE credibility than a White House press secretary is a sharp political joke dressed as an insult. Highly quotable, great economy.

jokes.text: He's gonna get his tits done next.
jokes.setup: The group has established Mike's vanity through his dyed mustache.
jokes.punchline: The logical escalation of male vanity is taken to an absurd physical extreme.
jokes.explanation: Solid button to the cold open sequence. Escalates well from cosmetic vanity to surgery. Economy is strong. Less original than the Cheetos/Yosemite Sam lines but functions as a good capper.

jokes.text: She's outlasted William Henry Harrison, our shortest serving president. / She feels great, unlike President Harrison who contracted pneumonia during his inaugural address and died.
jokes.setup: Mike is asked how the president feels about outlasting Harrison.
jokes.punchline: Mike delivers the gruesome historical death as a cheerful contrast to the president's good health — completely missing how low the bar is.
jokes.explanation: Works on two levels: the historical detail is itself funny (the PR team celebrating that she hasn't died yet), and Mike's earnest delivery of the grim contrast is perfectly in-character for an oblivious press secretary. High character integration.

jokes.text: Nice work, Mike. You're on fire. Nope, wait. It's your mustache.
jokes.setup: Sean sarcastically compliments Mike on his cheerful death-comparison PR answer.
jokes.punchline: The mustache callback pivots 'on fire' from metaphor to literal insult.
jokes.explanation: One of the tightest joke structures in the episode. The misdirection of 'on fire' as praise, the beat of 'Nope, wait,' then the pivot to the literal mustache — this is textbook Veep economy and timing. Exceptionally quotable. The structure alone earns a high craft score; it's the kind of line a writer's room would be proud of.

jokes.text: I call it Tangerine Dream.
jokes.setup: Mike has been mocked about his absurdly orange-dyed mustache.
jokes.punchline: Mike has named his mustache, and the name itself is both defensive and completely self-unaware.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly in character — Mike's complete lack of self-awareness is the joke. That he not only dyed his mustache but named it something he finds cool, and the name ironically confirms how orange it is, is a gem of character comedy. Four words, maximum payload. High quotability.

jokes.text: Yeah, if anyone can do this, Dan, I'm confident you or the guy we hired to replace you can.
jokes.setup: Dan is being told he's needed for the Families First bill negotiations.
jokes.punchline: The supposed vote of confidence reveals Dan is already being replaced — the confidence is in whoever replaced him.
jokes.explanation: A cutting passive-aggressive compliment that reveals a major plot point while being devastating to Dan's ego. The structure is excellent — builds to the knife-twist at the end. Very Veep in its cruelty disguised as encouragement.

jokes.text: Middle East, middle easy.
jokes.setup: The Israeli state visit is discussed as a major diplomatic event.
jokes.punchline: Selina's blithe dismissal of Middle East complexity via a rhyming non-slogan.
jokes.explanation: Three words that encapsulate Selina's breezy overconfidence perfectly. The wordplay is cheap but that's precisely why it's funny — she's casually rhyming her way through geopolitics. Strong character integration; only Selina would say this without irony.

jokes.text: I'm handling world peace, okay? You just go make the room pretty, i.e., the opposite of that fucking monstrosity. What is that? Picasso's heavy period?
jokes.setup: Someone is trying to discuss dinner centerpieces with Selina, who has just dismissed Middle East peace as easy.
jokes.punchline: She dismisses the person but immediately engages with the painting at length, calling it 'Picasso's heavy period.'
jokes.explanation: Works because of Selina's hypocrisy — she dismisses the décor person while immediately fixating on décor. 'Picasso's heavy period' is a decent art-world jab. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Is that why his wife tried to kill herself?
jokes.setup: Someone mentions that the previous president loved the ugly painting.
jokes.punchline: Selina casually implies the painting drove the former First Lady to a suicide attempt.
jokes.explanation: Genuinely dark and sharp. The logic is absurdist (the painting caused a suicide attempt) but delivered so matter-of-factly that it lands as wit rather than shock. Strong character integration — only Selina would make this leap so casually.

jokes.text: I think it's called 'A Man Who Shits Triangles.'
jokes.setup: The ugly abstract painting has been compared to Picasso's heavy period.
jokes.punchline: The title attributed to the painting is both crudely descriptive and somehow plausible as bad avant-garde art.
jokes.explanation: An inventive piece of absurdist naming comedy. The title sounds exactly like what a humorless modern artist would call something, and the scatological specificity makes it funnier. Works as a standalone joke but also retrospectively colors the whole painting thread. High originality.

jokes.text: 'I'm a latte'? Guess she means she's late.
jokes.setup: Amy texts in with what appears to be autocorrected 'late' as 'latte.'
jokes.punchline: The staffer reads it straight, then interprets it with a slight pause — the mundanity of autocorrect error as a workplace joke.
jokes.explanation: A competent quick joke about autocorrect — functional but not special. The Veep machine can do better. Gets a mild chuckle from the timing of the reveal.

jokes.text: You make me any more late, and I lock you in this car and have it compacted.
jokes.setup: Amy is stuck in traffic in a car with her driver.
jokes.punchline: Her threat escalates from frustration to vehicular homicide fantasy with complete calm.
jokes.explanation: Solid Amy character joke. The escalation from 'make me late' to 'compact you in a car' is disproportionate in the best Veep way. High character integration — Amy's controlled rage is a series signature.

jokes.text: Memo to self... it is winnable. / Delete the private polling. / Also, hair. Ask Marianne about electrolysis.
jokes.setup: Amy accidentally plays Selina's private voice memo in the car instead of music.
jokes.punchline: Three revelations in quick succession: Selina's secret confidence about winning, illicit private polling, and a personal note about electrolysis — each more private than the last.
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy with three escalating private revelations. The structure is perfect: political secret (winnable), professional misconduct (private polling), then the delicious personal indignity (electrolysis). The escalation from public to intensely private mirrors the power of the embarrassment. Strong character work revealing Selina's insecurities in three quick strokes.

jokes.text: No offense, honey, but, I mean, look at yourself.
jokes.setup: Catherine is complaining about bad press photos.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'no offense' preface followed immediately by 'look at yourself' to her own daughter — the qualifier makes it worse, not better.
jokes.explanation: A quintessential Selina-Catherine dynamic moment. The 'no offense' that precedes a deeply offensive statement to her daughter is cringeworthy in the best way. Perfect character integration for Selina's oblivious cruelty.

jokes.text: She turned around the college film society. / I transformed it, Mom.
jokes.setup: The group is trying to find Catherine a role, listing her skills.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's correction of 'turned around' to 'transformed' — she's insisting on the grandeur of running a college film club.
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical joke about Catherine's overinflated self-regard in minor accomplishments. The single word substitution ('transformed') as a character reveal is good writing. Works both as a character joke and as a setup for why she fails in the political world.

jokes.text: She... she... good for you. [Beat of awkward dismissal]
jokes.setup: Catherine corrects 'turned around' to 'transformed' about her film society achievement.
jokes.punchline: Selina's completely deflated, dismissive 'good for you' — the pause and trail-off communicate she couldn't care less.
jokes.explanation: Selina's dismissive mumble is a reaction beat that says everything about her relationship with Catherine without words. Good character integration; the trailing quality of 'she... she...' before the grudging acknowledgment is performed disdain.

jokes.text: I feel like I'm on trial in the future.
jokes.setup: Amy is appearing via a video call that is cutting out.
jokes.punchline: Selina describes the glitchy video as feeling like a futuristic trial — articulating the exact uncanny wrongness of early video call tech.
jokes.explanation: A genuinely clever observational line that captures the dystopian aesthetic of bad video conferencing. 'On trial in the future' is an inventive, specific description. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Uh, Amy, it's filtering out like every other word. Usually I just do that myself.
jokes.setup: The video call with Amy is cutting out so only some words come through.
jokes.punchline: Selina notes she usually tunes Amy out herself — implying the technology is merely automating her own indifference.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, self-incriminating joke that reveals Selina's relationship to Amy perfectly. She's not praising the technology — she's confessing she ignores Amy constantly. Great economy and structure; lands well because of the delayed self-awareness implied.

jokes.text: By the way, I'm bringing in Bill Ericsson. / Madam President, did you just say Bill Ericsson? / Ma'am, I... Ooh!
jokes.setup: Selina casually drops a major bombshell — she's replacing Amy with Bill Ericsson — in the middle of the glitchy video call.
jokes.punchline: Amy's audible shocked gasp/exclamation that cuts off mid-call, leaving the bombshell hanging.
jokes.explanation: The cut-off reaction is a well-constructed cringe beat — Amy's 'Ooh!' is funnier for being truncated. The casual way Selina delivers devastating news mid-meeting is signature Veep. Rewatch value because you see it coming on the second viewing.

jokes.text: Have you seen that thing? It's like he ate a volcano.
jokes.setup: Returning to Mike's mustache in a new scene.
jokes.punchline: New extended metaphor for the orange mustache — 'ate a volcano' is more violent and absurd than the Cheetos comparison.
jokes.explanation: The running mustache gag gets a new metaphor. 'Ate a volcano' is inventive and vivid, adding to the tapestry rather than repeating. The callback structure gives the running gag more texture.

jokes.text: But her likability index is... [Beat] ...shallow.
jokes.setup: Kent is building to explaining Catherine's unpopularity ratings, and has said 'likability index is...' with a long pause.
jokes.punchline: The one-word delivery of 'shallow' — clinical, brutal, devoid of compassion.
jokes.explanation: Kent's delivery of the single word 'shallow' after a pregnant pause is a masterclass in Veep comedy of bureaucratic cruelty. The pause implies something diplomatic is coming; 'shallow' is the opposite — it's as blunt as a diagnosis. Perfect character integration for Kent's data-driven, emotionless delivery. Maximum economy: one word does all the work.

jokes.text: Oh. Oh, these are not good. [Looking at Catherine's poll numbers]
jokes.setup: After Kent says Catherine's likability is 'shallow,' Selina is shown the actual numbers.
jokes.punchline: The doubled 'oh' and Selina's horrified understatement as she processes her daughter is actively disliked by the country.
jokes.explanation: A solid reaction beat. The 'oh. oh.' rhythm of someone absorbing bad news is funny in context, especially that her first reaction is clearly more political than maternal. Functional cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Don't tell me it's Happy Harrison Day. / I outlived some dead idiot president. Wow.
jokes.setup: Ben is building to tell Selina something special, and she assumes it's about Harrison.
jokes.punchline: Selina preemptively mocks the Harrison milestone she's supposedly celebrating as trivial.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the Harrison joke with Selina's characteristic dismissiveness. 'Outlived some dead idiot president' is punchy self-deprecation about the milestone her staff is celebrating.

jokes.text: It's Ben's birthday. / Happy birthday, Ben. / Wow, that was quick. / We have a cake? / No.
jokes.setup: The big interruption that Kent said was 'not just' peace talks turns out to be Ben's birthday.
jokes.punchline: The revelation that there is no cake — and the rapid-fire exchange deflating the birthday announcement.
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of this exchange is excellent Veep construction: big buildup by Kent → Ben's birthday → cheerful 'Happy birthday!' → 'Wow, that was quick' → 'We have a cake?' → flat 'No.' The deflation structure is precise, and the 'No' capper is perfectly timed. The cakelessness makes the already underwhelming birthday announcement sadder and funnier.

jokes.text: Ah, William Henry Harrison. Oh, God, at least somebody... [pause] Noticed this horrible cake?
jokes.setup: A cake finally appears — but it says 'William Henry Harrison' on it, presumably for the Harrison milestone.
jokes.punchline: Ben's relief that someone remembered him is immediately subverted — the 'somebody' they're relieved about is the cake's subject, not Ben.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed misdirect followed by a callback. Ben thinks someone remembered his birthday; instead they got a Harrison cake. The 'at least somebody...' pause is doing heavy lifting — we think he's going to be touched, and then it's crushed by 'Noticed this horrible cake.' The indignity of being upstaged by a dead president's cake on your birthday is very Veep.

jokes.text: Better think of a way to make me feel good. / Okay, of course. I could go get you some Pringles or...
jokes.setup: Teddy tells Jonah to make him feel good, implying a power dynamic.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's immediate, sincere offer of Pringles as a first instinct to make a superior feel good.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's Pringles response is perfectly calibrated to his character — obsequious and slightly dim, reaching for the most literal, mundane interpretation of 'make me feel good.' High character integration.

jokes.text: You simultaneously made my day and ruined his. Thank you. Shine brightly.
jokes.setup: Teddy tells Jonah he's been assigned to the Families First talks alongside Dan.
jokes.punchline: Teddy frames Jonah's inclusion as simultaneously good (for Jonah) and damaging (for Dan), then signs off with a bizarre 'shine brightly.'
jokes.explanation: 'Shine brightly' is a wonderfully odd sign-off for a political operative, and 'simultaneously made my day and ruined his' is an honest encapsulation of Washington politics. Solid character comedy.

jokes.text: Tap-tap-tap-tap-tap-tap. / All good? / Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Teddy has just finished talking to Jonah and pats him in a way that lingers uncomfortably.
jokes.punchline: The tap-tap-tap-tap-tap-tap rhythm of excessive patting, followed by a check-in that suggests the patting was a known act requiring consent.
jokes.explanation: This is uncomfortable because Teddy's tactile relationship with Jonah is played as vaguely inappropriate — the 'all good?' check suggests the patting is a known behavior. The rewatch bonus is high because in later episodes Teddy's abuse of Jonah is more explicit, making this retroactively darker.

jokes.text: If anybody doesn't know the difference between a valance and a jabot, I really need you to leave.
jokes.setup: Gary is coordinating the state dinner decor.
jokes.punchline: The absurd specificity of demanding window treatment literacy as a baseline requirement — Gary's world of obsessive domesticity.
jokes.explanation: Very Gary — his expertise in domestic luxury goods is a character signature. The demand for jabot knowledge as a prerequisite for participation in White House decoration is absurdly fussy and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Did we get these from a homeless man's grave? They're molesting my eyes.
jokes.setup: Gary sees arrangements he disapproves of at the state dinner.
jokes.punchline: Two wildly escalating insults — 'homeless man's grave' for the aesthetic and 'molesting my eyes' for the effect.
jokes.explanation: 'Homeless man's grave' is an inventive, specific, and darkly funny image for bad taste — implying things were literally scavenged from death. 'Molesting my eyes' is an unusual construction that escalates the offense. Strong dual-punchline structure.

jokes.text: Sure, Amy, I'll just tell the Israelis to move on, 'cause they love that.
jokes.setup: Amy demands to see the president right away, interrupting the Israeli state visit.
jokes.punchline: Sue's deadpan irony — suggesting she'll ask the Israelis to 'move on,' with the loaded implication that they really don't love being told that.
jokes.explanation: This is among the sharpest lines in the episode. Sue's understatement does double duty: it's funny as office comedy (the gatekeeper shutting Amy down) AND as geopolitical irony (telling Israelis to 'move on' in the context of a peace negotiation has layered meaning). The delivery — pure deadpan — is exactly right. Highly quotable. Strong character integration for Sue's specific brand of cutting efficiency.

jokes.text: I have a specific title, but a wide brief... Eliminate weaknesses. / That's a little Nazi doctor.
jokes.setup: Bill Ericsson is vaguely describing his new role at the White House.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'Nazi doctor' comparison is both an insult and an apt description of the vague, ominous role description.
jokes.explanation: 'Eliminate weaknesses' does genuinely sound like a Mengele job description. Amy's fast-draw comparison is sharp, and the punchline lands because Bill doesn't really deny it — just announces his title. Good economy.

jokes.text: Well, I just wanted to say a friendly hello in an unfriendly way.
jokes.setup: Amy has been hostile throughout her interaction with Bill Ericsson.
jokes.punchline: She summarizes the interaction with this perfectly self-aware oxymoron.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed line. The oxymoron ('friendly hello / unfriendly way') is self-aware and yet sincere — Amy knows exactly what she was doing and is fine with it. Maximum economy (10 words), perfectly in character, highly quotable. This is the kind of line that gets clipped and shared.

jokes.text: [beat] Hello.
jokes.setup: Amy has just delivered the cutting 'friendly hello in an unfriendly way' and walked away.
jokes.punchline: Bill's single-word response — 'Hello' — after a beat, to the empty air, is the deflated, understated button that confirms he's been thoroughly handled.
jokes.explanation: The perfect beat-delayed follow-up. Bill's 'Hello' to an already-departed Amy, after a pause, shows he's been destabilized. The joke is entirely structural — the word itself is neutral, the timing and context carry all the comedy.

jokes.text: I like him. And I loathe politicians.
jokes.setup: After the brief exchange with the Israeli PM, Selina comments on him.
jokes.punchline: She says she likes him — and that she hates politicians — without registering the obvious irony that she is a politician.
jokes.explanation: The self-exemption from the category she belongs to is classic Selina — she considers herself above the usual political muck. Good character integration. The irony is obvious but the delivery makes it land.

jokes.text: Madam President, a painting in the White House was removed this morning. / Should we go to the Situation Room?
jokes.setup: An aide gravely announces that a painting was removed.
jokes.punchline: Selina, whose job is genuine national security, asks 'should we go to the Situation Room?' — treating a painting removal with the same gravity as a military crisis.
jokes.explanation: A great escalation joke that parodies crisis-response culture. The aide's solemnity about a painting triggers Selina's Situation Room instinct, suggesting everything becomes a crisis in this White House.

jokes.text: I mean, who knew we even had one?
jokes.setup: Told that the removed painting was the only work by a Native American artist in the White House, Selina's response is about her ignorance of its existence.
jokes.punchline: Her first reaction to a politically sensitive Native American artifact being removed is surprise she had one — not remorse, not concern, just confusion.
jokes.explanation: Selina's inadvertent confession of complete ignorance about her own building is both a political joke (diversity as tokenism) and a character joke (Selina's incuriosity). Six words, sharp.

jokes.text: Actually, have Bill Ericsson do that because I'm having Bill Ericsson replace you.
jokes.setup: Selina tells her aide to issue a statement; then casually delivers the news that Bill Ericsson is replacing him.
jokes.punchline: The firing is slipped in as an afterthought, as if it's less important than the PR statement.
jokes.explanation: Selina delivers yet another career-ending announcement as an afterthought. The repetition of 'Bill Ericsson' in the same sentence is a structural choice that makes the firing feel parenthetical. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: To be honest, I don't even know if she's one of ours. / 20 bucks says she's Mossad.
jokes.setup: A mysterious woman is taking notes during the peace talks.
jokes.punchline: The president doesn't know who half the people in her own peace talks are, and casually bets she's an intelligence operative.
jokes.explanation: The Mossad bet is funny because it's entirely plausible, and Selina's comfort with not knowing who she's meeting with is a sharp joke about the chaos of her White House. Good absurdist quality.

jokes.text: Even though we have our differences, I think my mom brokering major peace talks is super awesome. / It is. Super awesome.
jokes.setup: Kent is leading Catherine into discussing her image, and she opens with this.
jokes.punchline: The phrase 'super awesome' — used completely sincerely by a grown woman about her mother and earnestly echoed by the data-driven Kent — is jarringly juvenile.
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'super awesome' with the gravity of the setting is funny. Kent echoing it is funnier. It sets up Catherine's immaturity before the poll number revelation.

jokes.text: I didn't take drugs with those guys. I was just in their bus for half an hour.
jokes.setup: Kent says he wants to talk about a 'personal matter,' and Catherine immediately confesses something.
jokes.punchline: She preemptively volunteers an incriminating admission that wasn't being asked about — but the self-defense makes it worse.
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect misdirection. Kent's 'personal matter' prompts Catherine to confess to drug-adjacent behavior nobody was asking about — and her defense ('I was just in their bus') is the exact kind of thing a guilty person says. The structure is textbook: anticipation, unexpected confession, self-undermining clarification. Highly quotable because it stands alone. Strong character work for Catherine as the perennial liability.

jokes.text: [Long pause] That is good to know.
jokes.setup: Catherine has just volunteered the drug bus confession.
jokes.punchline: Kent's pause and completely neutral 'that is good to know' — neither judging nor reassuring, just filing it away.
jokes.explanation: Kent's response is exceptional character comedy. The pause lets the confession marinate, then he responds with the most bureaucratically neutral phrase possible — 'that is good to know' — as if it's been filed under 'future intelligence.' The character integration is a 9.5 because only Kent would respond this way. Maximum economy: five words absorb all the comedy from the previous confession.

jokes.text: Catherine, America doesn't like you. / That sounded way too harsh when boiled down to a headline thought.
jokes.setup: Kent has been building diplomatically to deliver Catherine's poll results.
jokes.punchline: He delivers the brutal headline ('America doesn't like you'), then immediately self-critiques his delivery as too direct — but doesn't change the substance.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional cringe-comedy writing. Kent's self-awareness that 'America doesn't like you' was too blunt, delivered after the fact, is the perfect Kent joke — he's aware of the social conventions but couldn't stop himself from violating them. The second-guess without retraction is the exact shape of his character. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: They hate me. / I wouldn't say hate. You just... polarize opinion with the bulk of it gravitating to this pole here.
jokes.setup: Catherine responds to Kent's 'America doesn't like you' with 'they hate me.'
jokes.punchline: Kent's correction of 'hate' — he prefers to describe it as gravitating toward the negative pole — is diplomatic language that ultimately confirms the same thing.
jokes.explanation: The polspeak version of saying 'they hate you' while appearing to soften it. 'Gravitating to this pole here' — presumably while pointing at the bad end of a chart — is brutally funny because it accomplishes nothing. Kent character integration is outstanding.

jokes.text: You have sharp shoulders. / This is like high school all over again. / Yeah, sure, kind of, but much bigger.
jokes.setup: Catherine is reacting emotionally to the poll numbers.
jokes.punchline: Kent makes an irrelevant non-sequitur about her shoulder shape, then agrees that yes, this is basically like high school — 'but much bigger.'
jokes.explanation: The shoulder observation is a wonderful non-sequitur from Kent — he notices and says things other people would suppress. His agreement that it IS like high school, 'but much bigger,' is a brutally literal response that fails to comfort. Strong character work.

jokes.text: Customary shortcuts to public affirmation are military service or childbirth. / Okay, God, no, and, oh, my God, no, in that order.
jokes.setup: Kent presents the two fastest routes to public likability for Catherine.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's double rejection — 'God, no' for military, 'oh, my God, no' for childbirth — escalating each refusal to be funnier than the last.
jokes.explanation: The escalating double-refusal ('God, no' escalating to 'oh, my God, no, in that order') is a beautifully structured laugh. Catherine's horror at the options for gaining likability is both funny and sad. The tag 'in that order' is a perfect capper that shows she understood which was which. High quotability.

jokes.text: Well, then we go back to the idea of turning that frown into the inverse of a frown. / Upside down? / If you will.
jokes.setup: Kent is trying to offer an alternative route to likability since military and childbirth are ruled out.
jokes.punchline: He describes 'smiling' as 'turning that frown into the inverse of a frown,' and when Catherine says 'upside down?' he confirms 'if you will' — as if 'smile' is too informal.
jokes.explanation: A highlight of the episode. Kent refusing to say the word 'smile,' substituting 'the inverse of a frown,' then confirming 'if you will' when Catherine translates — is pitch-perfect character comedy. It captures everything about Kent's clinical, data-driven personality. 'If you will' is the best word in the exchange. Maximum character integration. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: So there wasn't even like a little part of you that thought I was gonna be in these talks? / Okay, look, just sit there in the meeting and let me take the lead. If I need your help, I'll give you a signal. / Okay, what's the signal? / There's not gonna be a signal, Jonah.
jokes.setup: Jonah is clearly expecting to be a major player in the negotiations; Dan is clearly planning to sideline him.
jokes.punchline: Dan offers a signal, then immediately revokes the signal — the joke is in the false hope and its instant erasure.
jokes.explanation: The signal-then-no-signal exchange is a small gem. Dan's 'I'll give you a signal' followed immediately by 'there's not gonna be a signal' is the most efficient possible way to convey their entire relationship. Jonah's sincere question 'what's the signal?' makes it land harder. Strong Jonah/Dan character work.

jokes.text: What's shakin', bacon? That's my way of saying hello, breaking the ice.
jokes.setup: Congressman Pierce enters the meeting.
jokes.punchline: He uses the phrase 'what's shakin', bacon' and feels the need to explain that it's his greeting — a sure sign it doesn't land.
jokes.explanation: A quick character-establishing joke. The self-explanation of a greeting is the tell — he knows it's not natural. Good minor character comedy.

jokes.text: I really like your office. This is a great office. The way you've arranged the furniture. This horse guy over here, that painting. This is the nicest office I've ever been in.
jokes.setup: Jonah is supposed to be helping Dan negotiate the Families First bill.
jokes.punchline: He spends his time gushing about the congressman's office decor instead.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's obsequious distraction is classic. The cumulative effect of his compliments — horse guy, furniture arrangement, the painting — builds into full absurdity. Strong character integration for Jonah's sycophancy masking incompetence.

jokes.text: Don't think of them as kids as much as little human start-ups. You know, spend to save. And then you take the money you save and you spend it. That's economics.
jokes.setup: Dan is pitching the educational investment argument for Families First.
jokes.punchline: His 'little human start-ups' metaphor is oddly convincing until the circular 'spend the money you save and spend it — that's economics' collapses the logic.
jokes.explanation: 'Little human start-ups' is genuinely inventive reframing language, and the circular economics logic ('spend the money you save and spend it') is a perfect parody of politician-speak that sounds meaningful while saying nothing. Good Dan character work.

jokes.text: And on my desk here, you see I keep a box of coupons. I clip them myself. And this is to help me to remember to be economical. / That is a fantastic idea. I'm gonna take a page. I'm gonna do this myself. / Did you know that a lot of these have expired?
jokes.setup: The congressman is illustrating his fiscal conservatism via a coupon box.
jokes.punchline: Jonah enthusiastically embraces the coupon idea, then immediately notices — and announces — the coupons have expired.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's catastrophic social intelligence failure — picking up the congressman's sacred coupon box, declaring he'll do the same, then immediately deflating the whole symbolic gesture by noting the coupons are expired — is perfect Jonah comedy. High character integration. The punchline lands hard because Jonah has no idea he's just undermined the entire meeting.

jokes.text: Well, you know, we're all getting together for a little hang at the White House bowling alley tomorrow night. Throwing strikes with the President of the United States.
jokes.setup: Dan is trying to get the congressman's support; Jonah spontaneously invites him to bowl with the president.
jokes.punchline: Jonah has no authority to make this invitation — and the phrase 'throwing strikes with the President of the United States' is an absurdly informal way to describe a presidential meeting.
jokes.explanation: Jonah freelancing a presidential bowling invitation he has no power to grant is good cringe comedy. The phrase 'throwing strikes' is charmingly inappropriate. The joke is in Dan's silent horror.

jokes.text: That's bucket list bowling.
jokes.setup: Jonah has offered White House bowling with the president.
jokes.punchline: The congressman's sincere delight at the idea — calling it 'bucket list bowling' — is both funny and characterizes him as easily bought.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'bucket list bowling' is inherently funny — bowling doesn't belong on bucket lists — and his sincerity makes it funnier. The easy corruption of a congressman by recreational sports access is very Veep.

jokes.text: She's probably not a very good bowler. You might even be able to beat her.
jokes.setup: Jonah is selling the bowling invitation.
jokes.punchline: He suggests the congressman might be able to beat the president at bowling — an invitation to defeat the most powerful person in the country — as a selling point.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's instinct to make the presidential bowling match appealing by suggesting the congressman could humiliate her is typically oblivious. Good character work.

jokes.text: We do have Native American staff. They're on a list that you're gonna give me in 30 minutes, okay? Now get me some scalps. Names. Get me some names.
jokes.setup: Selina needs to show respect for Native Americans after the painting controversy.
jokes.punchline: She asks for 'scalps' as shorthand for names — using a deeply insensitive term in a conversation about Native American relations.
jokes.explanation: The irony of using 'scalps' in the context of trying to patch relations with Native Americans is sharp political satire. Her immediate self-correction ('Names. Get me some names.') shows she caught it but too late. Very Veep — the gaffe contained in the damage-control effort.

jokes.text: Hey, I hate to ask, but would you mind helping me out? Would you tell the media that Bill Ericsson's taking your job? It would just make it seem more amicable. Would you do that for me? / [Long pause] No.
jokes.setup: Ben approaches Mike to ask for a favor.
jokes.punchline: The favor is that Mike announce his own replacement — and after a long pause, Mike's flat 'No' is both the obvious response and the funniest possible delivery.
jokes.explanation: The structure here is excellent. Ben builds through multiple self-deprecating 'hate to ask' hedges and qualifiers, making the request sound more and more absurd — and then the long pause followed by Mike's single syllable 'No' is perfect comedy timing. The 'No' has been set up by about 30 words of pre-apologizing. One of the episode's best exchanges.

jokes.text: It's not a wall, ma'am. / Well, it's an expression. A bridge of peace, I'll say.
jokes.setup: Selina used 'first brick in the wall of peace' — her aide corrects her given Israeli-Palestinian wall politics.
jokes.punchline: Selina pivots to 'bridge' without acknowledgment — just swaps one metaphor for the other while pretending nothing happened.
jokes.explanation: The 'wall' gaffe in Middle East peace talks is a sharp contextual joke — walls are the central controversy in Israeli-Palestinian politics. Selina's seamless pivot to 'bridge' while conducting the actual talks is a classic Veep damage-control-as-comedy beat.

jokes.text: Yes, it's ironic that you're talking to us about occupation while you occupy someone else's continent.
jokes.setup: Notes have been passed about the Native American painting controversy during peace talks about Israeli occupation.
jokes.punchline: The Israeli negotiator delivers a pointed observation that the US lecturing Israel about occupation while having occupied indigenous land is ironic.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the sharpest jokes in the episode — a geopolitical observation that lands as both irony and genuine critique. The callback structure (Native American painting → occupation accusation in peace talks) is elegant. The Israeli negotiator's cool delivery while Selina visibly squirms is the kind of political satire Veep does at its best. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: So why don't you tell Sacheen Littlefeather to get off the rag and get over it, all right?
jokes.setup: Selina steps out of the peace talks to address the Native American painting controversy.
jokes.punchline: She tells an aide to dismiss the Native American advocate using a crude, sexist, and historically insensitive reference — a triple-stack of political incorrectness in one sentence.
jokes.explanation: Selina's use of 'Sacheen Littlefeather' (the Native American activist who accepted Brando's Oscar) as a generic term for any Native American advocate, combined with 'get off the rag,' is layered political incorrectness that is simultaneously a character joke (Selina's ignorance/crudeness) and a political satire (how administrations actually talk behind closed doors).

jokes.text: It was just a preemptive cleaning, much like a juice cleanse. / But the Native American painting is the only one getting the cleanse? / Other paintings will also be cleansed. Cleansed is not the right word. Wiped. No, not wiped. / Cleaned.
jokes.setup: Mike is trying to explain away the painting removal.
jokes.punchline: His metaphor of 'juice cleanse' already sounds absurd; when pressed, he reaches for 'cleansed,' self-corrects to 'wiped,' realizes that's worse, and finally lands on 'cleaned' — the word he started with.
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of cringe escalation. Mike's word choices get progressively more loaded: 'cleaned' → 'cleansed' (religious/ethnic connotation) → 'wiped' (genocidal connotation) → back to 'cleaned.' The structure is near-perfect: each correction is worse than the last before he finds his way back to the start. Juice cleanse as a political metaphor for racism is genuinely inspired. One of the episode's best comedy sequences.

jokes.text: Gary, you look whiter than a Georgia country club.
jokes.setup: Amy sees Gary looking extremely pale and alarmed.
jokes.punchline: The comparison to a Georgia country club — not just 'white' but specifically a historically segregated Southern institution.
jokes.explanation: A strong, specific simile — 'Georgia country club' adds a layer of political resonance to what could have been a generic pallor joke. Amy's voice suits this kind of sharp, specific insult.

jokes.text: Is there a child missing?
jokes.setup: Gary says what he did is 'worse than' telling the president, implying severity.
jokes.punchline: Amy calibrates how bad it is with the most extreme possible marker — a missing child.
jokes.explanation: Four words that encapsulate Amy's pragmatic severity calibration. The jump from 'something bad happened' to 'is there a child missing?' as the reference point is sharp and very Amy.

jokes.text: I wanted her first state dinner to be really nice. So you what? Hired strippers?
jokes.setup: Gary is confessing he overspent on the state dinner.
jokes.punchline: Amy's hypothetical leap to 'hired strippers' as the logical explanation for spending too much money on a dinner.
jokes.explanation: Amy's absurdist jump is funny and character-consistent. The specificity of 'strippers' rather than a generic 'something bad' makes it land.

jokes.text: Gary, your inner child needs to grow an outer man.
jokes.setup: Gary is reluctant to tell the president about his mistakes.
jokes.punchline: Sue delivers this oddly constructed piece of motivational advice — mixing therapy language ('inner child') with a masculinity demand ('outer man').
jokes.explanation: One of Sue's best lines. The construction is clever — 'inner child needs to grow an outer man' mixes therapeutic jargon with macho imperative in a way that makes grammatical sense while being inventive. Sue's deadpan delivery would make it land harder. Very quotable. Strong character integration for Sue's blunt efficiency.

jokes.text: Boy, at peace with Israel, at war with Native Americans.
jokes.setup: The day's events have run in parallel — peace with Israel progressing, Native American controversy escalating.
jokes.punchline: The neat ironic summary is delivered as a dry observation.
jokes.explanation: A tight, economical callback that summarizes the episode's A and B plots in eleven words. The ironic juxtaposition is sharp — winning one geopolitical battle while losing a domestic one accidentally. High quotability and economy.

jokes.text: Do you know what I am? I'm like the opposite of Mel Gibson.
jokes.setup: Selina hears the ironic summary of her day — peace with Israel, war with Native Americans.
jokes.punchline: She self-identifies as 'the opposite of Mel Gibson' — meaning she's pro-Israel but anti-Native American (via the painting controversy).
jokes.explanation: This is the best observational joke in the episode. 'The opposite of Mel Gibson' is a perfect cultural shorthand — Mel Gibson is known for antisemitic rants, meaning the opposite is pro-Jewish but (implicitly) anti-indigenous. Selina says it with pride while being completely blind to the second half of that equation. Extremely quotable, high originality, perfect economy. This is what Veep does at its peak — political satire wrapped in a celebrity reference that has a sharp second layer.

jokes.text: Now what we need is a lot more butt-ugly Native American paintings, okay? / Because the first butt-ugly one was removed / by some jerkoff with a hair ball for a brain.
jokes.setup: Selina is furious about the painting crisis and what it cost her politically.
jokes.punchline: She calls for more 'butt-ugly Native American paintings' as a solution — the cultural respect entirely instrumental — while unknowingly insulting the person who moved it (Gary, present).
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony — Gary present while Selina calls the person who moved the painting 'a jerkoff with a hair ball for a brain' — adds a layer of cringe. 'Butt-ugly' cultural representation as the political solution encapsulates the show's cynicism perfectly.

jokes.text: She's got a lot on her plate right now, no pun intended. / That her bagman spends like a Babylonian king? Yes, I think so.
jokes.setup: The group is deciding whether to tell the president about Gary's spending.
jokes.punchline: The 'no pun intended' about a state dinner is followed by Ben's 'Babylonian king' characterization of Gary's spending.
jokes.explanation: Two jokes packed together. 'No pun intended' for the plate/dinner line is self-aware about a groan-worthy pun. 'Babylonian king' is a vivid image for lavish spending that elevates the insult. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: You can tell her. She likes you. / And I plan on keeping it that way, so I'll leave it to you.
jokes.setup: Passing the buck on who tells Selina about Gary's spending.
jokes.punchline: Ben's reason for not telling her is explicitly self-preservation — the honesty of his cowardice is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Ben's naked self-preservation reasoning is funny and true to character. The exchange builds toward a buck-passing chain that extends to Sue — good structural comedy.

jokes.text: Sue, would you like to tell the president? / No, I would not, but thank you for thinking of me.
jokes.setup: The buck-passing chain reaches Sue.
jokes.punchline: Sue's refusal is delivered with the politeness of declining a party invitation — 'thank you for thinking of me' is a perfectly dry social grace applied to a cowardly political situation.
jokes.explanation: Sue's 'No, I would not, but thank you for thinking of me' is exquisite. The application of formal social courtesy to a flat refusal to do someone's job is peak Sue comedy. The contrast between the polite register and the blunt content is perfectly constructed. Maximum character integration — this is the most Sue line in the episode. Extremely quotable. Among the episode's very best.

jokes.text: Beep, boop, beboop. Hello? / Yeah, hi.
jokes.setup: Jonah is introducing Richard to the White House hallway life.
jokes.punchline: Jonah uses robot/phone sounds to initiate a conversation — for no reason — and Richard gamely responds.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's randomness is his character trait — the robot sounds are very him. But the joke doesn't fully develop beyond 'Jonah is weird.' Functional.

jokes.text: You are in the J-Corps now and we own these hallways. / Hey, Preppy Longstocking. / Yeah, your fat mama.
jokes.setup: Jonah is showing Richard around the White House as if he's a tour guide of a territory he 'owns.'
jokes.punchline: A passing staffer insults him ('Preppy Longstocking'), and Jonah's comeback is a generic 'your fat mama' — a terrible comeback from a 6-foot-something adult man.
jokes.explanation: 'Preppy Longstocking' is a decent insult for Jonah (he is very tall and vaguely preppy). His 'fat mama' comeback reveals that despite his bravado, he has the verbal sophistication of a 12-year-old. Strong character integration for Jonah's social incompetence.

jokes.text: The president and I, we're like this. You know, not to scale, obviously.
jokes.setup: Jonah is claiming closeness to the president.
jokes.punchline: His self-deprecating 'not to scale, obviously' — acknowledging he's much bigger than the president — is the one moment of genuine self-awareness from him.
jokes.explanation: 'Not to scale, obviously' is a nice moment of Jonah self-awareness about his physical size relative to his claims of intimacy. A small but well-turned line.

jokes.text: I'm flying solo. I'm fucking Amelia Earhart and I'm still doing a better job than you.
jokes.setup: Jonah is defending his lone-wolf approach against Dan's team.
jokes.punchline: The Amelia Earhart comparison — someone famous for disappearing and dying — as a brag. The simile argues against itself.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jonah self-own that he doesn't recognize as such. Amelia Earhart as a role model for someone 'flying solo' is perfect — except she died doing it. Jonah invokes her with complete confidence. The unintentional self-undermining is precisely his character. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: [Catherine attempts to smile for the camera; Selina recoils] / That's not good. / You're flaring your nostrils.
jokes.setup: Gary and Selina are coaching Catherine on her photo face after the poll numbers.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's attempt at a smile is apparently so bad that Selina immediately recoils and notes nostril flaring.
jokes.explanation: A strong visual gag (we imagine Catherine's face) made funnier by Selina's specific diagnosis of the problem. 'Nostril flaring' is a specific enough detail to feel clinically observed. The visual payoff of the coaching session is a good callback.

jokes.text: You would not believe the day that I'm having. / Oh, what a day. / I feel like this is gonna be a key scene in my biopic.
jokes.setup: Jonah is reporting back to Teddy after the meeting.
jokes.punchline: Jonah genuinely believes he's living through a biopic moment — after a meeting where he embarrassed himself and got by on luck.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's self-mythologizing is a core character joke. 'Key scene in my biopic' after a meeting where he pointed out expired coupons and invented a presidential bowling date is perfect Jonah self-delusion. High character integration.

jokes.text: You got to make sure she has height here. A lot of volume because her skull is low. It's kind of indented.
jokes.setup: Gary is giving hair advice for Catherine.
jokes.punchline: Diagnosing Catherine with a 'low, kind of indented' skull while ostensibly giving styling advice.
jokes.explanation: Gary's medical-sounding skull assessment while giving hair advice is creepily specific and very funny. The clinical language ('kind of indented') applied to a family member's cranium is absurdly dark.

jokes.text: I think if there are three whole flower trucks parked outside the White House, I would know about it. / Go. Outside, go. Count the flower trucks.
jokes.setup: Amy doubts the scale of Gary's overspending.
jokes.punchline: There are enough flower trucks that someone needs to go count them — implying the number is surprisingly large.
jokes.explanation: Gary's profligacy being measured in flower trucks is a good visual joke. Selina's 'go count them' as a genuine task assignment is funny.

jokes.text: He's eager and hardworking and never complains and every night I dream of drowning him.
jokes.setup: Dan is describing Richard to Teddy, listing his positive qualities.
jokes.punchline: The list of virtues ends with Dan's recurring dream of drowning the earnest young man.
jokes.explanation: Textbook list-then-subvert comedy structure executed perfectly. Three positive character traits build an expectation of approval, then 'every night I dream of drowning him' arrives with maximum comedic force. The specificity of drowning (rather than a generic 'I want to kill him') and the dream-state delivery are excellent. Extremely quotable. One of Dan's best character moments.

jokes.text: Oh, well, I guess you'll meet them all at the Scorsese screening you're having in your head.
jokes.setup: Jonah complained that Dan has a team while he's flying solo; he invented a fake Scorsese screening to get a meeting.
jokes.punchline: Dan's dismissal of Jonah's imaginary film screening is a devastating callback to the lie.
jokes.explanation: A clean callback that punctures Jonah's bravado. 'In your head' is the perfect location for an imaginary screening. The line is economical and cutting.

jokes.text: I feel like this is gonna be a key scene in my biopic.
jokes.setup: Amy is standing on a chair for Wi-Fi in a hallway.
jokes.punchline: She frames her degradation as future biographical legend — a perfect self-delusion.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke. Amy's self-mythologizing while standing on a chair in a hallway to get Wi-Fi captures her ambition-vs-reality gap perfectly. 'Key scene in my biopic' is very quotable and specific to her character. Justifies 8.

jokes.text: I am sorry. There's a lot of Navajo in my district. This painting thing is escalating. I'm afraid I'll have to postpone. / About that screening with Marty... you ever see 'The King of Comedy'? / I haven't. / It's a good one. Underrated.
jokes.setup: The congresswoman is backing out because of the Native American painting controversy; Jonah has to acknowledge the Scorsese screening he invented doesn't exist.
jokes.punchline: Instead of coming clean, Jonah recommends 'The King of Comedy' — a Scorsese film about a delusional fan who believes he's close to a celebrity — as his exit.
jokes.explanation: This is among the smartest jokes in the episode. 'The King of Comedy' — Scorsese's film about a delusional fan who fantasizes about being close to a celebrity he has no real relationship with — is the perfect recommendation from Jonah, a delusional staffer who fantasizes about power he doesn't have. Whether or not Jonah intends the irony is ambiguous, which makes it funnier. Exceptional originality. The 'It's a good one. Underrated' capper is perfectly deadpan. High rewatch bonus because the full irony requires film knowledge.

jokes.text: Hey, sugar tits, did you stroke and choke the rules committee?
jokes.setup: Teddy checks in with Jonah about the lobbying results.
jokes.punchline: 'Sugar tits' as the greeting, 'stroke and choke' as the euphemism for political persuasion — Teddy's aggressively inappropriate language revealing his character.
jokes.explanation: Teddy's gross idiom is character-consistent (this is setup for a later season storyline). The euphemism as political jargon is functional. The 'sugar tits' greeting establishes his inappropriateness.

jokes.text: Sugar tits, did you stroke and choke the rules committee?
jokes.setup: Teddy greets Amy in the hallway.
jokes.punchline: The greeting 'sugar tits' — aggressively inappropriate — followed by the euphemistic 'stroke and choke' for political maneuvering.
jokes.explanation: Teddy's inappropriate language establishes his sexual harassment character (important for later seasons). 'Stroke and choke' as political terminology is a decent euphemism. Not the sharpest line but serves character-establishment purposes.

jokes.text: Richard apparently is the assistant's assistant. Does that mean, like, he's the epitome of an assistant or he's just an assistant's assistant? / The first one.
jokes.setup: Teddy is confused about Richard's title.
jokes.punchline: Richard's deadpan claim that 'assistant's assistant' means he's the 'epitome of an assistant' — a completely sincere and absurd interpretation.
jokes.explanation: Richard's earnest reframing of 'assistant's assistant' as the apex of a profession is both absurd and slightly inspiring. His two-word answer 'the first one' is perfectly timed. Good Richard character introduction.

jokes.text: Great, or at least between me and Teddy.
jokes.setup: Jonah is told he now has someone under him (Richard).
jokes.punchline: Jonah's 'or at least between me and Teddy' implies he's aware of something inappropriate in his relationship with Teddy and wants a buffer.
jokes.explanation: This joke has enormous rewatch value because Jonah's discomfort with Teddy's physical proximity becomes a much darker running thread in later episodes. 'At least between me and Teddy' is funny in context but devastating in retrospect. Good cringe comedy even without the future context.

jokes.text: Great, or at least between me and Teddy.
jokes.setup: Jonah is told he 'now has someone under you.'
jokes.punchline: 'Or at least between me and Teddy' — implying Teddy's inappropriate physical attention, with Jonah framing Richard as a human buffer.
jokes.explanation: Dark and character-specific. The implication that Jonah is using Richard as physical protection from Teddy is disturbing and funny simultaneously. The casual way it's dropped — and then immediately walked back with 'Hmm?' — is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Oh, 'sir.' I like that. I'll get used to that. / Keep doing it. / Yes, sir. / There it is again. That's twice. Come with me.
jokes.setup: Richard calls Jonah 'sir' for the first time.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's disproportionate delight — he's starved for respect — manifest in tracking each instance and ordering more.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's desperation for basic respect — counting 'sir' instances — is a sharp character joke. The instruction 'keep doing it' followed by 'that's twice' counting them like a miser counting money is very effective. 'Come with me' as an order delivered to his first-ever subordinate is satisfying.

jokes.text: What's Gary doing? Trying to max out America? / Wow, who knew they made lampshades out of unobtanium?
jokes.setup: The group is discovering the extent of Gary's spending.
jokes.punchline: 'Unobtanium' — a fictional material from Avatar — as a reference for the most expensive, rarest possible lampshade material.
jokes.explanation: Unobtanium is an okay pop-culture reference for rare/expensive. 'Trying to max out America' is a funnier line. Both are decent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Okay, guys, I will not hear a single bad word about my friend Gary. / But, no. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, he's out of control.
jokes.setup: Selina initially defends Gary.
jokes.punchline: She immediately abandons the defense with 'But, no. Yeah. Yeah.' — a cascade of self-contradiction.
jokes.explanation: The speed with which Selina reverses on Gary's defense is perfectly timed. 'But, no. Yeah. Yeah.' is the exact verbal texture of someone talking themselves out of a position they never really held. Good character work.

jokes.text: Did you see the cost of the dinner? This'll sting us, make us look decadent and remote. / Yeah, said the Princeton grad in the Valentino tux.
jokes.setup: An aide worries the expensive dinner will make them look out of touch.
jokes.punchline: Ben turns the concern back on the aide — pointing out he's a Princeton grad in a Valentino tux, i.e., also decadent and remote.
jokes.explanation: A very sharp political observation — the people most worried about appearing elitist are themselves elite. The specificity of Princeton + Valentino tux makes it land. Ben's economy is excellent here.

jokes.text: See that? / It's kind of how we do things here in 'the Wing.' / Now through this door is the Oval Office. / Fudge me. No. / Yeah.
jokes.setup: Jonah is doing a power tour for Richard.
jokes.punchline: 'Fudge me. No.' — Richard's authentic disbelief at the Oval Office door, and his choice of 'fudge' as an expletive substitute.
jokes.explanation: Richard's 'fudge me' is a character-specific expletive — clean, earnest, and disarming. His genuine awe at the Oval Office while with Jonah is funny by contrast (the worst possible guide).

jokes.text: Now through this door is the Oval Office. / Fudge me. No. / Yeah. / You are like the coolest guy I've ever met.
jokes.setup: Jonah shows Richard a door near the Oval Office.
jokes.punchline: Richard's 'Fudge me. No.' — a G-rated expletive in a profanity-saturated show — is perfectly funny, and his declaration that Jonah is 'the coolest guy I've ever met' sets up future irony.
jokes.explanation: 'Fudge me' in the middle of an episode full of f-bombs is a wonderful character note for Richard — his exclamation is completely authentic and stands out. 'You are like the coolest guy I've ever met' addressed to Jonah Ryan is also inherently funny given the audience's knowledge.

jokes.text: God, I hope you don't have any allergies, though, 'cause these centerpieces are kind of imposing, aren't they?
jokes.setup: The state dinner flowers arrive — implied to be enormous given Gary's extreme overspending.
jokes.punchline: Gary's understatement ('kind of imposing') about centerpieces so large they could trigger allergies confirms they are absurdly oversized.
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the 'three whole flower trucks' setup — the centerpieces are so enormous they are a medical hazard. Rewatch bonus because on screen these would presumably be comically oversized. Gary's earnest pride makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Better not leak any photos. I see your media likes to make a big fuss about how much you spend. / Yes. Yes. Huh? What? What fuss? What spending?
jokes.setup: The Israeli PM casually mentions that the media makes a big deal about overspending.
jokes.punchline: Selina's panicked stall — 'Yes. Yes. Huh? What? What fuss? What spending?' — is the comedy of someone trying to play dumb about something they know very well.
jokes.explanation: The transition from 'yes' agreement to 'Huh? What?' panic is brilliantly timed character comedy. Selina's verbal fumble on the world stage with the Israeli PM is perfectly cringe.

jokes.text: Well, imagine Elton John on a day he feels fat.
jokes.setup: Selina asks how much Gary has spent.
jokes.punchline: Ben's answer is not a number but an image — Elton John's spending habits on a self-consciously indulgent day.
jokes.explanation: One of the most quotable lines in the episode. The Elton John image is brilliantly specific — he's known for outrageous spending — and the added detail of 'on a day he feels fat' implies retail therapy at maximum scale. The decision to give a cultural reference rather than a dollar figure is a great comedy writing choice. High originality and economy.

jokes.text: Did we ever find out who moved that painting, by the way? / Um, that would be Gary, too.
jokes.setup: In the middle of processing Gary's spending scandal, Selina asks about the painting.
jokes.punchline: The painting removal — the other major scandal — also turns out to be Gary.
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Gary is responsible for both scandals is a well-constructed escalation — the painting crisis and the spending crisis combine into one Gary crisis. The 'that would be Gary, too' phrasing, so matter-of-fact, is excellent. Sets up the confrontation perfectly.

jokes.text: So I have just brokered a peace deal with Israel here. I, as President of the United States, have actually achieved something, which is virtually unheard of.
jokes.setup: Selina has achieved a genuine diplomatic victory; she's now being told her bagman ruined it.
jokes.punchline: Her self-aggrandizing description of a historic achievement, followed by the devastation that it will be overshadowed by a paint-and-flowers scandal, is pure Veep.
jokes.explanation: 'Which is virtually unheard of' is a good self-aware joke about how rarely American presidents achieve actual peace deals. Selina's pride in doing her actual job is funny precisely because it shouldn't be remarkable. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Who do you think you are, Gary Antoinette? Did somebody make you first lady? 'Cause I don't remember marrying you, Gary. I don't remember fucking you in Niagara Falls. I think I'd remember that.
jokes.setup: Selina has found Gary hiding after discovering he's responsible for both scandals.
jokes.punchline: She escalates from 'Gary Antoinette' to imagining she married him to referencing a specific sexual encounter at Niagara Falls she definitely didn't have.
jokes.explanation: This is the comedic peak of the episode and one of Veep's finest Gary-Selina confrontations. The escalation is structurally perfect: 'Gary Antoinette' (historical insult about delusion and excess), 'first lady' (inverting their roles), 'I don't remember marrying you' (escalating the marriage metaphor), then the devastating and absurd 'fucking you in Niagara Falls' — the specificity of Niagara Falls as a honeymoon destination makes it simultaneously ridiculous and somehow plausible as a Selina insult. 'I think I'd remember that' is the capper that brings the house down. This is Selina's voice at its most unhinged and specific. Extremely quotable, high craft, exceptional structure.

jokes.text: You have suckered onto me like some sort of a car window Garfield.
jokes.setup: Selina is telling Gary how dependent he is on her.
jokes.punchline: The comparison of Gary to a suction-cup Garfield car toy — specifically the ones that cling to windows — is a wildly specific and inspired image for parasitic dependence.
jokes.explanation: One of the most original insults in the series. 'Car window Garfield' is both deeply specific (everyone knows those suction-cup Garfield toys) and perfectly apt as a metaphor for Gary's clinging, decorative, slightly pathetic attachment. The specificity elevates it beyond any generic 'parasite' insult. This is the kind of line that gets memorized and quoted. Among the best lines in the episode by a significant margin. Craft is exceptional.

jokes.text: You are not a big shot, Gary. You're a middle-aged man who sanitizes my tweezers.
jokes.setup: Gary is defending his importance to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina reduces his entire existence to one specific, intimate, and diminishing task — sanitizing tweezers.
jokes.explanation: The choice of 'sanitizes my tweezers' as the defining description of Gary's role is brilliant. It's a real task (he absolutely does this), it's intensely personal and hygiene-related (intimate but not romantic), and it reduces him to a servant of her most trivial vanity. The specificity — not 'fetches my coffee' or 'carries my bags' but this — is outstanding writing.

jokes.text: When's Catherine's birthday? / June eighth. / Ninth. / Which senator's daughter is in rehab? / You're out of line, missy. / Geldray. / What are you wearing tomorrow? / I don't know. / I do.
jokes.setup: Gary pushes back against Selina's dismissal by demonstrating the depth of his knowledge about her life.
jokes.punchline: Each answer Gary provides is more intimate and damning than the last — he knows things Selina doesn't know about herself, up to knowing her schedule better than she does.
jokes.explanation: The rapid-fire quiz format with escalating intimacy is beautifully structured. Catherine's wrong birthday, the senator's daughter in rehab (later confirmed as Geldray — itself a callback), and the 'what are you wearing tomorrow / I don't know / I do' sequence — the last exchange is the sharpest, showing Gary knows her future better than she does. Exceptional character integration for both Gary's dependence and Selina's reliance on him.

jokes.text: When's Catherine's birthday? / June eighth. / Ninth. / Which senator's daughter is in rehab? / You're out of line, missy. / Geldray. / What are you wearing tomorrow? / I don't know. / I do.
jokes.setup: Gary is defending himself against Selina's claim that he's unimportant.
jokes.punchline: His three-point demonstration of irreplaceable knowledge: Catherine's birthday (wrong by one day, immediately corrected), the senator's daughter in rehab, and what Selina is wearing tomorrow.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's structural masterpieces. Gary proves his value through three increasingly intimate pieces of knowledge, with the first one being immediately corrected (June 8th vs 9th) — which both undermines and confirms his point (he knows it better than she does). The escalation from birthday to rehab secrets to her wardrobe for tomorrow is perfect. 'I do' at the end lands like a mic-drop. Strong callbacks to earlier information (Geldray). Justifies 8.8.

jokes.text: I'm your calendar, I'm your Google, I'm your Wilson the volleyball.
jokes.setup: Gary is defending his indispensability to Selina.
jokes.punchline: He escalates from practical functions (calendar, Google) to 'Wilson the volleyball' — the inanimate companion from Cast Away that Tom Hanks talked to in isolation.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'calendar' → 'Google' → 'Wilson the volleyball' is a masterpiece of three-part comedy structure. The first two are functional and diminishing (he's a tool); the third is devastating and poignant — he's her only companion, an inanimate object of emotional dependency. The Wilson reference captures both the depth of Gary's attachment and Selina's isolation. Extremely quotable, exceptional character integration. Among the best lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Can you find somebody else who did what I did? / You mean on Labor Day? / I didn't say that. / Yeah, you did. You just did. You j... you just said Labor Day.
jokes.setup: Gary asks if anyone else could do what he does; Selina interprets this as a reference to something specific — on Labor Day.
jokes.punchline: The implication that something happened between Gary and Selina on Labor Day — possibly sexual, definitely inappropriate — is never clarified, making it funnier.
jokes.explanation: The Labor Day implication is one of the best cringe beats in the episode. The joke works because it's never explained — the vagueness is the joke. Selina's immediate connection of Gary's statement to a specific incident implies there IS something, and her 'I said I would never mention that, ever' confirms it. The show trusts the audience to fill in the blank. Gary's denial ('I didn't say that') while Selina insists he did is perfect. Extremely high rewatch bonus — this gets funnier the more you wonder what happened.

jokes.text: I'm sorry if I lost my temper a little bit. / I am so sorry for the words that I just spoke.
jokes.setup: Selina has just delivered one of her most devastating Gary tirades.
jokes.punchline: Her immediate walk-back is a hilariously insufficient apology — 'lost my temper a little bit' and 'words that I just spoke' are profound understatements for what she said.
jokes.explanation: 'Words that I just spoke' is a good euphemism for 'I compared you to a car window Garfield and implied we had sex at Niagara Falls.' The understatement is well-calibrated.

jokes.text: Can you find somebody else who did what I did? / You mean on Labor Day?
jokes.setup: Gary asks if anyone else could do what he does.
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate specific reference to 'Labor Day' — implying a specific incident too dark or intimate to name — which Gary instantly regrets raising.
jokes.explanation: The episode's funniest and most structurally brilliant joke. 'Can you find somebody else who did what I did?' → 'You mean on Labor Day?' The joke tells us everything while telling us nothing. We never learn what happened on Labor Day, and that's exactly why it's perfect — the audience's imagination fills it with something appropriately terrible. The specificity of 'Labor Day' (a date, not just 'that time') implies both a real event and that both parties know exactly what it was. Gary's immediate 'I didn't say that / Yeah, you did' reveals that he regrets opening this door. This is textbook Veep: the humor lives entirely in what isn't said. Comparable to the best jokes on television. Justifies 9.3.

jokes.text: That looks good. / Yeah, it's... It's light sponge. / Would you like a piece? / Okay, I'll have a piece, I guess.
jokes.setup: After the devastating confrontation, Gary offers Selina cake.
jokes.punchline: The complete tonal whiplash — from the most intense argument of the series to 'would you like a piece of cake?' — and Selina's casual 'I'll have a piece, I guess' as if nothing happened.
jokes.explanation: The pivot to birthday cake after the confrontation is perfect tonal comedy. The joke is entirely structural — the same birthday cake that had no significance becomes the vehicle for reconciliation after the most intense exchange in the episode. 'It's light sponge' and then 'you just told me that' on the second mention — the conversation returns to normalcy so fast it's funnier than any punchline could be. Among the episode's best moments for craft.

jokes.text: That looks good. / Yeah, it's... It's light sponge. / Would you like a piece? / Okay, I'll have a piece, I guess. / Yes, ma'am. / It's light sponge. / You just told me that. / Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: After the enormous confrontation, Selina and Gary are looking at the Harrison cake together.
jokes.punchline: The complete deflation — they're back to discussing cake. Gary tells her 'it's light sponge' twice. She notes he's already said that. He 'mm-hmms.' The total domesticity of the aftermath.
jokes.explanation: The aftermath of the most intense scene in the episode — mutual silence over cake — is beautifully constructed. 'It's light sponge' twice is the textbook Veep reset: they've said everything and now there's nothing to say. The relationship continues because it has to. The 'Mm-hmm' is the perfect button. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: It's light sponge. / You just told me that.
jokes.setup: Gary tells Selina the cake is light sponge; she takes a piece; he tells her again.
jokes.punchline: 'You just told me that' — Selina's flat reminder that Gary repeated himself, immediately after they've just shared an emotional earthquake.
jokes.explanation: Four words that do enormous comedy work. The normalcy of Selina correcting Gary on a minor repetition — immediately after he declared himself her Wilson the volleyball — is a perfect button. Their relationship just snaps back. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: Geldray's daughter is in rehab? / Yes. / That explains all that energy. / Lot of energy.
jokes.setup: The Geldray daughter information Gary provided during his outburst is now being processed.
jokes.punchline: Selina's explanation that rehab explains the daughter's energy — a dark, judgmental, and politically savvy read — with Gary's echoing 'lot of energy.'
jokes.explanation: A good callback that confirms the Geldray intel is already being weaponized. 'That explains all that energy' is a perfectly dark political read. Gary's echoing is sweet and characteristically servile.

jokes.text: I just find these paintings to be inspirational. I really do. / I particularly love this color here. I think this is so pretty, don't you? / I believe the title is 'Massacre.'
jokes.setup: Mike is staring admiringly at a Native American painting as a PR gesture after the controversy.
jokes.punchline: The painting he finds so 'pretty' and 'inspirational' is titled 'Massacre.'
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed callback and escalation. Mike's PR performance of appreciating indigenous art — 'I particularly love this color' — is devastated by the title 'Massacre.' The joke operates on multiple levels: Mike's ignorance, the irony of finding a massacre inspiring, and the continuing Native American storyline finding its perfect dark button. Excellent structure. Very quotable.

jokes.text: My, you are small. I suppose you're young, but you're still very small.
jokes.setup: Catherine is giving out medals at a ceremony as her new official role.
jokes.punchline: Her very first comment to a child receiving a medal is about their size — twice.
jokes.explanation: Catherine is so socially awkward she leads with a child's stature. The doubling down ('but you're still very small') makes it land harder. The irony that this is her debut in her new 'likable' public role makes it a perfect payoff to the Kent plotline.

jokes.text: Honey, don't look so worried. I'm not gonna bite you. But that man over there does have a gun.
jokes.setup: Catherine is trying to comfort a nervous child at the ceremony.
jokes.punchline: She reassures the child she won't bite them, then points out the Secret Service agent's gun — an immediate terror escalation.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional cringe comedy. Catherine's attempt to calm a child by reassuring them she won't bite, then immediately pointing to an armed guard, is a perfect example of someone who is constitutionally incapable of comforting anyone. The structure is flawless: reassurance followed by immediate greater threat. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Honey, don't look so worried. I'm not gonna bite you. / But that man over there does have a gun.
jokes.setup: Catherine is reassuring a nervous child.
jokes.punchline: She reassures them she won't bite — then immediately points out that a nearby security person has a gun. The reassurance making the child more alarmed.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best Catherine joke. The structure is perfect: reassurance ('I'm not gonna bite you') → counter-productive addition ('but that man does have a gun'). Catherine's complete inability to read a room — telling a child not to worry about her while pointing out armed security — is a brilliant character comedy moment. 'But' is the key word: it functions as a reassurance that immediately undermines itself. Justifies 8.5.

jokes.text: That's a wonderful photo of your daughter. / Yeah, she looks so loveable there, don't you think? / Very... well, she looks likable. / Do you think she looks likable in that picture?
jokes.setup: Selina is watching Catherine at the ceremony, noting how she photographs in this context.
jokes.punchline: The pivot from 'loveable' to 'likable' — the exact metric that Kent told them was Catherine's problem — and Selina's anxious double-check.
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the likability index thread. The word substitution from 'loveable' to 'likable' is precise — we understand that Selina's mind is now filtered through the polling data. Her anxious 'do you think she looks likable?' reveals she's using her daughter instrumentally.

jokes.text: Have you ever seen 'Star Wars'? This is a little bit like the end of 'Star Wars.'
jokes.setup: Selina is watching Catherine hand out medals at the ceremony.
jokes.punchline: She compares her daughter's medal ceremony to the end of 'Star Wars' — the throne room scene — partly out of pride, partly out of delusion about its grandeur.
jokes.explanation: A decent character joke — Selina seeing her daughter's minor event as equivalent to a triumphant film climax. The deflation of Catherine's actual performance (the child comments) makes the Star Wars comparison retroactively funnier.

jokes.text: South Korea has called. They'd like to know if you'd like a baby elephant. / I have no response to that. / Other than I already have one. / I'm kidding.
jokes.setup: An aide delivers a completely unexpected diplomatic inquiry about a baby elephant.
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'I have no response to that / other than I already have one / I'm kidding' is a three-step joke that escalates from confused to absurd to self-deprecating.
jokes.explanation: The baby elephant question is perfectly absurd as a diplomatic inquiry. Selina's 'I have no response to that' is the correct and funny response. 'Other than I already have one' implies Catherine is the baby elephant, making 'I'm kidding' necessary — but the fact that she had to add it makes it funnier. Strong three-part structure.

jokes.text: South Korea has called. / They'd like to know if you'd like a baby elephant. / I have no response to that. / Other than I already have one. / I'm kidding.
jokes.setup: An aide delivers diplomatic correspondence in the middle of the state dinner.
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of a baby elephant offer as diplomatic currency; Selina's 'I already have one / I'm kidding' as a joke that doesn't land even to herself.
jokes.explanation: The baby elephant diplomatic offer is absurdist, and Selina's failed attempt at a joke — 'I already have one / I'm kidding' — is a meta-joke about Selina's sense of humor. The 'I'm kidding' trailing off is well-observed.

jokes.text: She used to be chubby when she was little, but she's slimmed down as she's gotten older. Thank goodness.
jokes.setup: Selina is talking to the Israeli PM's wife about Catherine at the ceremony.
jokes.punchline: 'Thank goodness' — delivered as a sincere aside — is a devastating maternal judgment about Catherine's childhood weight.
jokes.explanation: Selina's 'thank goodness' about her daughter's past chubbiness — said to a foreign dignitary at a formal state dinner — is peak Selina. The inappropriateness is both politically and parentally terrible. High character integration.

jokes.text: Can you pull me out the second it becomes acceptable to leave?
jokes.setup: Selina has just watched her daughter's ceremony and commented on it with diplomatic awkwardness.
jokes.punchline: Even at her own daughter's triumphant public moment — a moment she engineered for political benefit — Selina's first instinct is to escape.
jokes.explanation: A good button on the episode. Selina wanting to escape her daughter's ceremony is a fitting final character beat — she engineered this whole thing and still can't stand to be there. Good character integration, slightly generic as a closer.

jokes.text: Can you pull me out the second it becomes acceptable to leave? / Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Selina has just achieved a historic peace deal and is hosting a state dinner.
jokes.punchline: She immediately wants to leave — asking Gary to extract her at the earliest socially acceptable moment. The monumental achievement is a chore.
jokes.explanation: The perfect episode button. After everything — the peace deal, the Gary confrontation, the light sponge cake — they're back to their baseline dynamic: Selina wanting to leave somewhere and Gary managing her exit. The episode's reset to normal is both funny and quietly perfect.

jokes.text: Can you pull me out the second it becomes acceptable to leave? / Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Selina has achieved a peace deal but is now trapped in the social obligations of the state dinner.
jokes.punchline: Her request to be extracted 'the second it becomes acceptable' — delivered quietly, like a prisoner asking about escape routes — is the perfect button on the episode.
jokes.explanation: Good closing character note. Selina's inability to enjoy any of her achievements — she brokered peace and now just wants to leave the dinner — is quintessential Veep. The staffer's 'mm-hmm' as if this is a completely normal request completes it.

tmdb_overview: The president's staff prepares for her state visit with the Israeli prime minister.
insight: Early season political chaos hits 95 jokes in 35 minutes—Veep's densest episode yet.
